title: Training CLIP
concept: CLIP is trained using a symmetric cross-entropy loss that maximizes the cosine similarity of matching image-text pairs along the matrix diagonal.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the benefit of prompt templates in zero-shot classification?

options.text: They match the full-sentence context CLIP saw during pre-training.
They compress the image patches to save memory.
They bypass the text encoder entirely.

exercises.explain: Correct! Sentence context helps CLIP extract cleaner embeddings than isolated keywords.

cards.title: Aligning the diagonal
cards.body: If you have a batch of 100 images and 100 captions, how do you mathematically train the model to link them correctly? You construct a massive grid of similarity scores. We want the correct matches along the diagonal to glow bright, while the incorrect matches everywhere else stay dark. Let's see how.

cards.title: Symmetric Cross-Entropy Loss
cards.body: CLIP is trained in batches of size N. We compute a similarity matrix of size N x N containing pairwise cosine similarities. The model is optimized using a symmetric cross-entropy loss: we treat the diagonal (matching pairs) as target labels, computing cross-entropy loss along both the rows and the columns.

cards.title: A seating chart
cards.body: Imagine a wedding with 50 couples. You draw a grid of seats. Row 1 belongs to Groom 1, Column 1 belongs to Bride 1. You want Groom 1 to sit next to Bride 1 (diagonal). You want to maximize the connection score along this diagonal, while keeping Groom 1 as far away as possible from all other brides.

cards.title: Matrix visualization
cards.body:
In a batch of 3:
Images: [Img1, Img2, Img3]
Texts: [Txt1, Txt2, Txt3]
The loss forces the similarity of (Img1, Txt1), (Img2, Txt2), and (Img3, Txt3) to reach 1.0, while (Img1, Txt2) and other combinations push to 0.0.

cards.title: No pixel reconstruction
cards.body: You might think CLIP must decode images or translate text. In reality, CLIP **never generates pixels or text** during training. It only computes similarity scores between vectors, which makes it extremely fast to train compared to generative models.

cards.title: CLIP Similarity Matrix
cards.body: How similarity matrix gradients are optimized:

exercises.prompt: How does CLIP calculate its training loss for a batch of size N?
exercises.explain: Symmetric cross-entropy optimizes the rows and columns of the similarity matrix to maximize diagonal alignments.

options.text: Using symmetric cross-entropy loss over an N x N similarity matrix of image-text pairs
By calculating the MSE between original and predicted pixels
By adding the weights of the two encoders directly
By running K-means clustering on the text tokens

exercises.prompt: True or false: Off-diagonal cells in the CLIP similarity matrix represent negative training pairs.
exercises.explain: True. Off-diagonal cells mismatch images and texts in the batch, representing negative targets to push apart.
The diagonal cells link matching image-text pairs, which are the positive samples.
exercises.sentence: CLIP's symmetric loss maximizes similarity scores along the ___ of the image-text similarity matrix.

exercises.prompt: Why does CLIP compute cross-entropy loss along both the rows and the columns?
exercises.explain: Symmetric loss ensures that we optimize both for finding the right text given an image, and the right image given a text.

options.text: To make the classification loss symmetric: matching text-to-image and image-to-text simultaneously
To double the speed of the GPU calculations
To normalise the absolute coordinate positions

exercises.prompt: What would happen if the temperature parameter scaling the cosine similarities in CLIP was kept too high during training?

options.text: The similarity scores would become too uniform, preventing the cross-entropy loss from identifying and sharpening the diagonal matches.
The model would automatically convert into a generative GAN.
The sequence length would shrink to zero.
The GPU would crash due to arithmetic overflow.

exercises.explain: Excellent! A learnable temperature scales the dot products, ensuring matching pairs stand out clearly. Next: CLIP applications and our Phase 11 capstone review!
